title: Credit Score (CIBIL) — Complete Guide
meta_description: CIBIL score guide 2026 — what is a credit score, how to check for free, how to improve from 600 to 750+, and why it matters for loans and credit cards.

hero.one_liner: Your 3-digit CIBIL score (300-900) determines whether banks approve your loan, what interest rate you get, and which credit cards you're eligible for

sections.heading: Overview
content: Your credit score (most commonly referred to as CIBIL score in India) is a 3-digit number between 300 and 900 that represents your creditworthiness — essentially, how likely you are to repay borrowed money. It's calculated by credit bureaus (TransUnion CIBIL, Experian, Equifax, CRIF High Mark) based on your credit history: loan repayments, credit card usage, and other financial behavior.

A score of 750+ is considered good and gets you the best loan interest rates and premium credit cards. 700-749 is fair — you'll get loans but may not get the lowest rates. Below 700, many banks will reject your application or charge significantly higher interest. Below 600, getting any credit is very difficult.

Your CIBIL score affects: home loan approval and interest rate (even 0.5% difference on a ₹50 lakh loan means ₹7+ lakh extra over 20 years), personal loan approval, credit card eligibility (premium cards like Infinia, Regalia need 750+), car loan rates, and even some job applications (financial sector companies check credit scores).

You can check your credit score for FREE once a year from each credit bureau. CIBIL offers free score at myscore.cibil.com, Experian at experian.in, and Equifax through its portal. Many apps like Paytm, CRED, and BankBazaar also show your score for free (updated monthly).

rows.label: Score range
rows.value: 300-900. Higher is better. 750+ is considered good.

rows.label: Factors
rows.value: Payment history (35%), Credit utilization (30%), Credit age (15%), Credit mix (10%), Recent inquiries (10%)

rows.label: Free check
rows.value: Once/year from each bureau (cibil.com, experian.in). Also via Paytm, CRED, BankBazaar.

rows.label: Update frequency
rows.value: Bureaus update your score monthly based on data reported by lenders

rows.label: Minimum history
rows.value: Need at least 6 months of credit history for a score to be generated. New-to-credit shows '-1' or 'NH'

sections.heading: How to Improve Your CIBIL Score — Actionable Steps
content: 1. Pay all EMIs and credit card bills ON TIME: Payment history is 35% of your score. Even one late payment can drop your score by 50-100 points. Set auto-pay for minimum amount on credit cards (to avoid late fees) and full EMI payments on loans.

2. Keep credit card utilization below 30%: If your card limit is ₹1 lakh, try not to use more than ₹30,000 at any time. High utilization signals financial stress to the algorithm. If you regularly max out your card, ask for a limit increase or get a second card to spread usage.

3. Don't apply for too many loans/cards at once: Each application triggers a 'hard inquiry' which temporarily drops your score by 5-10 points. Multiple inquiries in a short period signals desperation. Space out applications by at least 3-6 months.

4. Maintain a healthy credit mix: Having both secured (home loan, car loan) and unsecured (credit card, personal loan) credit shows you can handle different types of borrowing. Don't take unnecessary loans, but having at least one credit card with good usage history helps.

5. Don't close old credit cards: Credit age (how long you've had credit accounts) matters. Closing an old card reduces your average credit age and available credit limit — both hurt your score. Keep old cards active with occasional small purchases.

6. Check your report for errors: Sometimes bureaus have incorrect data — a paid loan shown as outstanding, or someone else's loan appearing on your report. Dispute errors at the respective bureau's portal. Correcting errors can boost your score immediately.

Timeline: If your score is below 700, these steps can bring it to 750+ in 6-12 months. Consistency is key — one good month doesn't fix years of poor habits, but sustained good behavior steadily improves the score.

steps.title: Check your free CIBIL score
steps.description: Visit myscore.cibil.com → register with PAN + personal details → get your score and full credit report.

steps.title: Review your credit report for errors
steps.description: Check all loan accounts, credit card accounts, and payment history. Dispute any errors through the bureau's dispute resolution process.

steps.title: Implement improvement steps
steps.description: Set up auto-pay, reduce credit card utilization, avoid unnecessary loan applications. Track score monthly.

steps.title: Monitor regularly
steps.description: Use free monthly updates from Paytm, CRED, or bank apps to track improvement.

faqs.question: I've never taken a loan or credit card. What's my score?
faqs.answer: You'll have 'No History' or '-1' score — which means the bureau has no data on you. This is different from a low score but also makes it hard to get credit. Solution: Get a secured credit card (deposit-backed) or become an add-on card holder on a family member's card. After 6 months of usage, you'll generate a score.

faqs.question: How long does it take to improve a bad score?
faqs.answer: From 500 to 700: 12-18 months of consistent good behavior. From 650 to 750: 6-12 months. From 700 to 800: 3-6 months. The key is zero missed payments and low credit utilization. There are no shortcuts — companies claiming to 'fix' your score quickly are scams.

faqs.question: Does checking my own score lower it?
faqs.answer: No. Checking your own score is a 'soft inquiry' and does NOT affect your score. Check as often as you like. Only 'hard inquiries' (when a lender checks your score for a loan/card application) can temporarily lower it.

faqs.question: My CIBIL and Experian scores are different. Which one matters?
faqs.answer: Different bureaus may have slightly different scores because they use different algorithms and may have different data (not all lenders report to all bureaus). Most Indian banks primarily use CIBIL. If your CIBIL score is 750+, you're good for most lending decisions.

faqs.question: I settled a loan for less than the full amount. How does it affect my score?
faqs.answer: Loan 'settlement' (paying less than owed) severely damages your score — it remains on your credit report for 7 years and is a red flag for all future lenders. Settlement is different from 'closure' (paying the full amount). Always try to pay the full outstanding amount rather than settling.

disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only. Consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.
